text: Two trocars are placed in the right lower quadrant, and a third trocar is positioned above the umbilicus for camera access. The sigmoid colon is visibly attached to the lateral abdominal wall, limiting the perforation. The surgeon begins dissection from the uncomplicated side of the colon.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery to dissect the mesocolon, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric artery, ligates it with two hemoclips, and then cuts the artery.
The left ureter is visible in the surgical field. The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein near the ligament of Treitz.
The surgeon identifies a second venous structure adjacent to the inferior mesenteric vein, which appears to be the renal vein, and safely removes it from the dissection site.
The surgeon ligates the mesenteric vein with hemoclips and then cuts it using an energy device.
The surgeon continues dissection on the lateral side, mobilizing the sigmoid colon. A perforation in the colon is revealed during the process.
Following intraoperative colonoscopy, the surgeon determines the resection margin for healthy colon mucosa and uses a stapler to transect the colon at this site.
The surgeon performs a colorectal anastomosis using a circular stapler. An air leak test is conducted, showing no leaks.
